turns.text: Hello Alejandrina, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for having me. Honestly, it’s a little surreal to be sitting on this side of the desk. For years, I’ve been the one holding the clipboard, explaining the "whys" and "hows" of early gestation to families in my classes. I thought I knew what to expect, but sitting in that exam room last week, seeing that "medium risk" flag on my chart... it hit me like a physical weight. My brain immediately started mapping out the potential complications, the odds, the physiological hurdles. It’s hard to turn that part of me off.

I’m a single mom-to-be, and living here in Boston, everything feels like it’s moving at a hundred miles an hour. When I heard the heartbeat—that fast, galloping rhythm—I didn't think, "Okay, that’s a normal range for this gestational age." I just started crying. It was the first time in weeks I felt like I could actually exhale. I’m trying so hard to let go of the "health educator" persona and just be a woman who is growing a life, but the fear of something going wrong is always lurking at 3:00 AM. It’s a strange, heavy, beautiful contradiction. I’m ready to talk, though. I think it might actually help to put some of this into words instead of just analyzing it. Where would you like to start?

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

Honestly? I think I expected a very neat, linear path—the kind I’ve laid out for my students a thousand times. I imagined myself walking into the clinic, confident and informed, asking all the right questions about prenatal vitamins and screening timelines. I thought the medical support would feel like a partnership of equals, since I already speak the language. I guess I naively assumed that having all that knowledge would act as a buffer against the anxiety.

But the reality has been… messy. It’s not just a clinical appointment; it’s an emotional gauntlet. The support I’m getting is excellent technically—my midwife is truly wonderful—but the "support" I actually *feel* is totally different from what I expected. I didn’t anticipate how much I would struggle to stop "performing" the patient role. I find myself mentally grading the care I receive, or suppressing my own fears because I feel like I *should* know better. 

It turns out, knowing the physiology doesn't make the vulnerability any less intense. The hardest part has been trying to stop being the professional in the room. I’m finding that what I really need isn’t just information—I have plenty of that—but a space where I can stop being the expert and just be someone who is, frankly, pretty scared and overwhelmed.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I think I fell for the illusion that being "prepared" was the same thing as being emotionally ready. Because I spend my days teaching the biology of it—the way the placenta anchors, the way hormones rewire the brain—I genuinely believed I’d be immune to the rollercoaster. I expected to feel this grounded, serene sense of purpose. I imagined I’d just glide through the first trimester with a clipboard and a healthy snack, taking it all in stride.

The reality? It’s been a total ambush. The physical exhaustion is one thing—the kind of bone-deep fatigue that makes you feel like you’re walking through deep water—but the emotional toll is so much sharper. Instead of the calm, clinical confidence I anticipated, I feel incredibly fragile. The moment that "medium risk" tag appeared on my chart, that sense of control I valued so much just evaporated. I realized that knowing exactly how a heart forms doesn't actually stop you from holding your breath every time you wait for the doppler to find the rhythm. 

I feel like a fraud half the time, honestly. I know the stats, but at 3:00 AM, those numbers offer zero comfort. I’m not as ready as I thought I was, and I think I'm still figuring out how to be okay with that uncertainty.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

Before I got pregnant, I looked at those apps and websites through a filter of professional necessity. I’d point my students toward them, thinking, *“Great, this is a reliable, data-driven resource.”* I expected them to be like textbooks: clear, objective, and reassuring. I thought the internet would be a place where I could look up a symptom, find the biological mechanism, and check it off a list.

But now that I’m actually *in* it? It’s a total minefield. When you’re the one experiencing the nausea or that persistent, nagging worry about a pregnancy being "medium risk," those same resources stop feeling like neutral information. They feel cold and almost alienating. Every time I open an app, I’m flooded with these little cartoons and lighthearted tips that feel like they’re meant for someone else—someone who isn’t worried about whether their body is doing its job.

What I’ve found is that the information isn’t the problem; it’s the lack of humanity. I don't need another list of, say, how many centimeters the fetus is growing this week. I need someone to say that it’s okay to be terrified when you’re doing this on your own. The apps are great for the facts, but they’re completely silent on the actual, messy, emotional reality of carrying a child. They offer data, but they don't offer any real comfort.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I think I expected the "system" to be proactive—a sort of hand-off where a primary care provider or specialist would sit me down, review my history, and say, "Okay, Alejandrina, here is the roadmap for how we’re going to manage this so you can focus on being excited." I expected a clear, coordinated plan that treated my health and my pregnancy as one cohesive story. I wanted that reassurance that someone else was holding the map, leaving me free to just experience the pregnancy.

In reality, it felt more like I had to be my own project manager. When I sought help, the experience was fragmented. I found myself having to bridge the gaps between providers, repeating my history over and over, and—ironically—often being the one to connect the dots between my medium-risk indicators and the specific care I needed. 

It wasn’t that the care was bad, but it felt reactive rather than proactive. I felt like I was navigating a labyrinth where I had to keep pointing out the walls myself. I wanted a partner in this, but I ended up feeling like I was the one steering the ship while simultaneously trying to patch the leaks. It’s exhausting to advocate for your own care when you’re already trying to just keep your head above water.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

It definitely wasn't a case of the system proactively wrapping me in support. If anything, I felt like I had to be the one to bridge the gaps. Because of my background, I think I was perhaps more vocal—or maybe just more *insistent*—than the average patient, but that was born out of necessity, not choice. I found myself having to connect the dots between my history and the current risk factors, making sure no one missed the nuances of my chart. It’s draining to have to keep one foot in the "patient" role and the other in the "advocate" role.

It felt like a series of disjointed hand-offs. I’d be in one office, then another, and I was the constant thread that had to weave it all together. I wanted to just *be*—to just be a woman pregnant with her second child—but instead, I felt like a glorified administrative assistant for my own medical file. I wasn't just receiving care; I was curating it. I think that’s what’s really stripped away the joy of these early months. When you’re constantly looking over your shoulder to ensure your care is being managed correctly, it’s hard to find the headspace to just sit with the miracle of it all.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

That’s a hard question to answer, because there isn’t a partner in the picture for me. I’m doing this on my own. 

Before I decided to pursue this, I think I had a very traditional, idealized vision of what that involvement should be. I imagined the shared excitement, the late-night discussions about names, and that sense of having someone else in the room to process the anxiety with. I expected a partner to be the one to help me keep my cool when the "what-ifs" started spiraling. You know—the person who would hand you the crackers when the nausea hits or just sit with you while you process the weight of it all.

The reality is that the silence in my apartment at 3:00 AM is deafening. There is no one to share that initial flicker of fear with, or to celebrate the heartbeat with, other than myself. I have to be my own sounding board, my own cheerleader, and my own protector. It’s isolating in a way I didn't fully grasp until I was actually sitting there, staring at the ultrasound monitor alone. I feel strong most days, but there are moments—especially when the exhaustion hits—where I really miss the idea of having a teammate. It makes the need for a truly supportive, attentive medical team feel even more vital, because they’re the only ones witnessing this with me.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

I think the burden has been total, and to be honest, it’s been incredibly heavy. Because I am doing this on my own, every single decision—from finding the right clinic to navigating the financial logistics of a single-income household—falls squarely on my shoulders. 

But it’s deeper than just the "to-do" list. The burden of *emotional* preparation is what’s really crushing. Because I know the medical side of things, there’s this unspoken pressure to be the "perfect, informed patient." I feel like I have to prove that I know what I’m doing, which makes it almost impossible to be vulnerable. When I’m in the clinic, I’m constantly editing myself, holding back the questions that sound too clinical so I don't intimidate the staff, or hiding my anxiety so I don't look like I'm "too much."

What drives this? Part of it is the fear of being perceived as "the single mother who doesn't have it together." I feel like I have to work twice as hard to be seen as stable and capable. I’m constantly balancing the medical knowledge in my head with the raw fear in my heart, and there isn't another person to help me carry that weight. I’m not just managing a pregnancy; I’m managing a reputation, a career, and a future all by myself. It’s a lot for one person.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I haven't had a loss, but I think that fear is always the shadow in the room for anyone who understands the biology. Even without that history, every single time I walk into that clinic, my mind automatically goes to all the ways things could stop working. It’s an occupational hazard—I know too much.

When I started this journey, I expected the healthcare system to acknowledge that anxiety. I wanted someone to say, "I know you know the stats, but we’re going to help you stay grounded." I expected that they would treat the *fear* as a real, tangible clinical issue, just as important as my HCG levels or my blood pressure. 

But in reality, that support is largely absent. The focus is almost exclusively on the physiological metrics. They check the box for "patient education," and they look for the heartbeat, but there’s no space carved out for the person who is losing sleep over the possibility of a problem. I’m essentially left to manage my own emotional turbulence. It feels like the system is designed to catch a problem if it *happens*, but it isn't set up to prevent the psychological toll that comes with the constant waiting. It’s a very lonely, sterile kind of care, even when the providers are kind.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

I actually haven't experienced a loss, but I can tell you that for me, the *anticipatory* grief is just as persistent. Even without that specific history, just knowing the statistics—knowing the "wrong" way things can go—creates a phantom loss that I feel every single day. 

When I mention my anxieties, or when I try to express that I’m struggling to balance my medical background with my personal fear, I often feel like the providers dismiss it as "just" first-trimester jitters or assume that because I’m a professional, I’m somehow immune to the trauma of the "what-ifs." It’s like they see my chart and think, *“She’s a health educator; she knows the risks and she knows the protocol, so she’s fine.”*

But that professional armor is just that—armor. It doesn't mean I’m not terrified. There have been times I’ve left an appointment feeling like my emotional state was treated as an afterthought, or even a nuisance. It’s isolating to have your expertise used as a reason to withhold the deep, human reassurance you’re actually craving. I don't need a lecture on why the first trimester is unstable; I need someone to recognize that I am a human being standing on that threshold, and I’m scared to death.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I expected something like a transition into a sanctuary. In my mind, I pictured soft lighting, a quiet room, and a midwife who would sit with me for a long, unhurried time to talk not just about the "medium risk" label, but about what it meant to be a person carrying this pregnancy on my own. I expected a space where my background would be a bridge to deeper, more sophisticated conversations—like a collaboration of minds.

The reality was much more clinical and, frankly, rushed. I walked into a brightly lit, sterile room that felt more like a transit station than a place of transformation. The air was thick with the scent of antiseptic, and the appointments felt chopped into fifteen-minute blocks. There was so much focus on the electronic health record—my midwife was constantly tapping on her tablet, making sure all the data points were logged correctly. 

I felt like I was being processed through a checklist rather than being seen as a human being. The information I received was technically sound—I already knew most of it—but the delivery felt perfunctory. I wanted to talk about my fears, to acknowledge the fragility of it all, but the environment felt too hurried to accommodate anything beyond the vital signs. I left feeling like I’d just passed an audit, not like I’d been cared for.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise—and honestly, the most painful one—was how much I had to suppress my own voice to keep the appointment moving smoothly. I expected that being a peer in the field would grant me a more nuanced, conversational relationship with my midwife. I thought we’d be able to have a "colleague-to-colleague" shorthand that would bypass the basic scripts.

Instead, I was surprised by how intensely lonely it felt to *not* be allowed to be a patient. Every time I tried to steer the conversation toward my real, visceral fears, I felt like the staff defaulted back to a clinical baseline, almost as if my background acted as a wall between us. It was a negative surprise: I felt "managed" rather than supported.

On the flip side, the positive surprise was that moment of grace when we found the heartbeat. I wasn’t prepared for how much that sound would shatter my internal "professional" persona. I expected to be analytical—to notice the tempo, the consistency, the sound quality. But when it started galloping, the health educator in me just vanished. I wasn't listening to a biological marker; I was listening to a miracle. The tears that came were the only thing that felt real and unscripted in that entire office. It reminded me that despite all the data and the checklists, there is still something profoundly sacred happening here, even if the clinical environment keeps trying to turn it into a procedure.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected a dialogue. I imagined sitting down with my midwife to review the results, where she would walk me through the raw data, acknowledging the clinical significance while also checking in on me—the human being. I thought we’d look at the numbers together, like a team assessing a project. Given my background, I expected to be treated as a participant in the interpretation, not just a passive recipient of a pre-digested summary.

The reality was far colder and significantly more automated. It felt like I was interacting with a black box. My results arrived in a patient portal—that sterile, blue-tinted interface—usually late in the evening when I was already staring at the ceiling, unable to sleep. It was a digital "ping" on my phone, devoid of context, tone, or warmth. There was no one to explain the nuances in real-time. I had to read the report, parse the lab values myself, and then wait days for a brief, boilerplate message from the office staff confirming that "everything is within normal limits." 

It was jarring. Being told via a screen that everything is "fine" doesn't actually stop the anxiety, especially when you know exactly what those numbers imply on a molecular level. I felt like the information was being rationed or sanitized, and it left me feeling incredibly isolated in my own clinical curiosity and fear.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

Oh, absolutely. There is a massive, cavernous gap, but it’s not for lack of data—it’s for lack of *meaning*. 

Because of my background, I don't need a simplified brochure that explains how a chromosome works. I know that. What I *need* is for someone to bridge the space between the sterile, raw data points on that portal and the reality of my life. When I look at those lab results, I’m seeing the mathematical probability of a struggle, and I desperately want someone to look at me—not my chart—and help me contextualize that. 

The system treats information like it’s a commodity to be dispensed in equal amounts to every patient, regardless of their own health literacy or personal history. So, I get the same, bland, "everything is within normal limits" script that everyone else gets. But when you’re a medium-risk patient with a head full of medical knowledge, that answer feels hollow. I’m left to interpret the grey areas myself, which is the last thing I want to be doing at 2:00 AM. I need a mentor, not a data entry clerk. I need someone who can see past the HCG levels and acknowledge the human being who is trying to survive the sheer, overwhelming terror of the unknown. That’s the information I’m actually looking for, and it’s nowhere to be found.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I expected—or maybe I should say, I desperately hoped for—a "lead" provider. Someone who would hold the thread of my narrative through the entire nine months. I imagined that by choosing a midwifery-led model here in Boston, I was opting into a relationship-based approach where I wouldn't have to re-explain my anxieties or my "medium risk" nuances every single time I walked through the door. I wanted a partner who would know my face, my history, and my fears without having to scroll through a tablet to find them.

The reality has been more like a relay race where I’m the only one keeping the baton. I’ve seen three different clinicians in just the first trimester. Every time, I have to perform the same "introduction" dance: explaining my professional background, my specific risk factors, and my emotional state, all while the provider rushes to catch up with my file. It’s like being a case study that everyone has to read for the first time while I’m sitting there, exposed.

There is no continuity of spirit, just continuity of documentation. They all have access to my chart, sure, but no one seems to *know* me. I feel like I’m constantly being handed off, which makes that "medium risk" label feel like a flickering neon sign that no one is actually watching. It's profoundly lonely.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It’s exhausting. Practically, it turns every appointment into a recurring audition. I spend the first ten minutes of every visit "re-performing" my own medical history—explaining why my chart is flagged, why I’m concerned, and trying to assert my agency before the provider has even looked at the physical metrics. By the time I’ve caught them up to speed, the appointment is half over, and there’s no room left for the deeper, more complex conversations I’m actually starving for.

Emotionally, it’s corrosive. I feel like a case file, not a person. When you see a new face every time, you lose the chance to build the kind of trust where you can finally let your guard down. I have to stay "on"—I have to be the professional, the educator, the informed patient—because I don’t know if this particular provider will understand that my anxiety isn’t pathological, but a reaction to my reality. 

If I had one midwife who knew my story, I wouldn't have to translate my fear into medical terms just to be taken seriously. I’d be able to just say, "I’m terrified," and have them understand the weight of that. Instead, I’m constantly managing the professional-patient divide, and I leave every office feeling more isolated than when I walked in.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I think I expected—and maybe naively hoped for—a discussion that centered on the *integration* of my body and my baby. As a health educator, I’m used to explaining the physiological mechanics, so I expected to have conversations that were grounded in that reality but treated with a sense of wonder. I thought they would ask about how I was *feeling* in my body, not just about the metrics.

The reality, though, is that the physical examination feels deeply disconnected from the person inside the body. It’s all about the measurements: the fundal height, the blood pressure, the weight. It’s clinical, it’s swift, and it’s devoid of any acknowledgment of the profound, often uncomfortable, transition I’m actually experiencing. When they check my abdomen, it’s for data, not for connection. 

It feels like my body has been abstracted into a series of symptoms and milestones to be documented. There’s no space to talk about the sheer, bone-deep exhaustion or the way it feels to have my digestive system slow to a crawl, unless those things show up as a clinical "issue" that needs fixing. I feel like an observer in my own clinical trial, and that distance—between the physical exam and the lived experience—is exactly what makes me feel so lonely despite being surrounded by "experts."
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was a specific moment during a routine scan when the sonographer was looking at the neural tube development. I already knew, clinically, what they were looking for—the precision, the anatomy, the markers. But when the tech went silent and spent what felt like an eternity focusing on one specific, shadowy angle of the spine, my heart just plummeted. 

They didn't say a word. They didn't explain what they were looking for, or *why* they were lingering, or even just check in to see if I was holding my breath. That silence felt like a canyon. I know the protocol; I know that silence is meant to be professional and focused. But in that moment, I wasn't a health educator observing a scan. I was a mother, terrified for her baby. 

When I finally asked, "Is everything okay?" they gave me a very brisk, "Everything looks standard so far," and moved on. It left me feeling completely dismissed. It wasn't about the *result*—which was fine—it was about being treated as if my fear was an inconvenience to their workflow. I wanted someone to hold space for the fact that even if I know the anatomy, I don't know the *outcome*. Being treated like an object that just happens to be carrying a fetus—it was deeply dehumanizing, and it left me shaken for the rest of the day.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I haven't hit those specific complications yet—thank God—but just having that "medium risk" flag on my chart feels like walking around with a target on my back. Before this, I imagined that if I were to develop something like gestational diabetes, the "care" would look like a team approach. I pictured a nutritionist, a midwife, and maybe even a counselor sitting down to build a manageable, low-stress plan that empowered me to keep things stable. I thought it would be a collaborative effort where I felt supported in my body’s struggle.

But based on what I’ve seen so far, the reality is likely just another layer of cold, procedural management. I expect they would hand me a glucometer, a stack of impersonal charts, and a strict, punitive list of dietary "don'ts," and then tell me to log everything into a portal. There’s no space in that system for the emotional weight of feeling like your body is failing you or failing your baby. 

If I end up facing those complications, I’m terrified that the system will just treat the glucose levels as a set of variables to optimize, while the person—me, the exhausted, anxious, single mother—is completely ignored in the process. It’s not the diagnosis I fear; it’s the clinical isolation that comes with it.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

It’s the *acknowledgment* that’s missing. You know, I have enough information. If I’m struggling to understand a lab result or a physiological shift, I can look it up in a textbook or access the medical literature. I don't need another pamphlet on prenatal nutrition. 

What’s truly missing is the human element—the bridge between the data and the person. I need someone to recognize that behind the "medium risk" flag and the textbook knowledge is a woman who is genuinely terrified. When I sit in that exam room, I’m not just a set of vitals or a repository for medical history. I’m a woman who is navigating this entirely on my own, often at 3:00 AM, in the quiet of an apartment where there is no one else to hold the weight of these fears. 

I don't need more "educational materials." I need a provider who will pause, put the tablet down, look me in the eye, and say, "I see you, and I hear that this is hard." I need someone who acknowledges that even if I understand the science of pregnancy, I am still allowed to feel the vulnerability of motherhood. That kind of emotional safety? It’s completely absent. It feels like the system is optimized for everything *except* the person having the baby.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected a system that functioned like a safety net—someone I could call, or a quick portal message that would get a thoughtful, nuanced response. After all, I’m in Boston; we’re surrounded by world-class hospitals. I assumed that if I had a question that wasn't strictly an emergency but was still keeping me awake at 3:00 AM, there would be a protocol for that.

The reality? It feels more like a brick wall. When I have a concern, I go through this automated triage. I leave a message with an answering service or send a portal note, and I’m essentially left in limbo. When I finally get a response, it’s usually from a nurse or a different midwife who doesn't know my history, and they give me the standard, "If you aren't bleeding or having severe pain, you're fine."

It’s that "fine" that kills me. They aren't addressing my concern; they’re just checking a box to dismiss the inquiry. It makes me feel like I have to wait for a disaster before I’m allowed to receive real care. I shouldn't have to be in crisis to get a human conversation. It’s like the system is designed to keep patients *away* unless there’s an absolute emergency, and for someone like me, who is already carrying this weight alone, that lack of responsiveness is just another layer of isolation.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Honestly? Yes, and it’s a constant, internal negotiation. Because I work in this field, I know the protocols, and I know exactly how overburdened the clinics are. I find myself playing "medical triage" on my own symptoms at 2:00 AM, debating whether my worry is "clinically significant" enough to justify a call or a message. I don’t want to be "that" patient—the one who the nurses roll their eyes at because she’s over-analyzing every twinge. 

There have been moments where I’ve felt a sharp, sudden cramp or an unusual period of exhaustion, and I’ve intentionally sat on it for twelve or twenty-four hours, just to see if it passes. I tell myself, *“Alejandrina, you know this is likely just the uterus expanding or low blood sugar. Don't waste their time.”* 

It’s a toxic cycle. I’m avoiding the very people who are supposed to be caring for me because I’m afraid of being judged as an anxious, high-maintenance professional. It’s pathetic, really. I should be the one advocating for the best possible care, but instead, I’m pathologically worried about being a burden. The system makes you feel like an intrusion rather than a partner, and that makes it incredibly easy to just stay silent and suffer through the uncertainty alone.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I think I held onto this fantasy that because I’m in a city like Boston, surrounded by colleagues and friends, I wouldn't actually be *alone* in this. I expected my circle to be a safety net of shared knowledge and emotional bandwidth. I imagined long, messy brunches where we’d talk about the reality of the first trimester, or having someone to lean on when the exhaustion became too much to manage on my own.

The reality, however, is that everyone has their own life, their own career, their own set of pressures. People are supportive in the abstract—they send a polite "How are you feeling?" text—but when you’re navigating a medium-risk pregnancy as a single mother, those casual check-ins don't bridge the gap. I’ve realized that most people, even those who love me, find it hard to hold the space for the sustained, 3:00 AM level of anxiety I'm living with. 

I don't think they know how to show up when the fear is less about "being pregnant" and more about the clinical reality of the potential complications. It’s not that they don't care; it’s just that they don't have the context. I’ve ended up feeling like I have to "manage" their expectations of my pregnancy, too—putting on a brave face, saying "I'm doing great," so they don't worry. It’s an exhausting performance that I wish I didn't have to keep up.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think I expected a village, or at least a steady stream of hand-me-downs and late-night calls. Instead, the support that *didn't* materialize was the deep, consistent emotional presence. People offer to buy me a cute onesie or send me a link to a generic "what to expect" article, but no one really asks the uncomfortable, messy questions. No one says, "I know you're terrified, and it’s okay to not be the professional today." They see my career and my intelligence, and they assume I’ve got it all under control. That assumption is, in itself, a form of isolation.

What I *did* find, surprisingly, was that the most reliable support came from the unlikeliest of places: a casual acquaintance who had been a single mom herself. She didn't offer advice or talk about my "medium risk" stats; she just showed up at my door one evening with a hot, home-cooked meal and didn't ask a single question about the baby’s development. She just sat on my couch and let me vent about how hard it is to wake up feeling like you’re failing at everything. 

That wasn't the kind of "support" I thought I needed—I thought I needed medical guidance—but it was the only thing that actually made me feel like I wasn't doing this in a vacuum. It turns out, I didn't need more information; I needed someone to acknowledge that I was drowning.

It’s funny, I’ve spent my whole professional life telling parents that the first trimester is just about "building the foundation." We use such sterile language—the neural tube closing, the heart chambers dividing. We teach it like it’s a factory process. But sitting here in my apartment now, I realize how much that mindset has failed me. It’s not just a process; it’s an existence. 

I’m struggling with the fact that I am simultaneously the most informed person in the room and the most terrified. I know the HCG doubling times. I know what a subchorionic hematoma looks like on an ultrasound. But none of that helps when I’m staring at the ceiling in the dark, wondering if my body is enough. 

I think the biggest lesson I’ve learned—or am still learning—is that I have to stop being my own clinician. When I go to my next appointment, I think I’m actually going to say it out loud: "I don't need a medical explanation. I need you to know I’m scared." If I don't give them permission to treat me like a vulnerable human instead of a colleague, I’m going to spend the next two trimesters in this same state of high-functioning misery. It’s time to stop the performance. I’m just Alejandrina. And for the first time, that has to be enough.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Oh, absolutely. It happens constantly, and it’s always delivered with this patronizing, "Well, you know how these things go, Alejandrina" tone. Because I work in health education, people—especially the clinical staff—often assume that my anxiety is just "over-knowledge" or "professional curiosity." They treat my fears like they’re just academic observations, rather than the raw, desperate nerves of a woman who is terrified for her child.

There have been moments in my appointments where I’ve tried to raise a specific concern, and before I’ve even finished my sentence, they’ve already pivoted to a clinical explanation, effectively cutting me off. It’s like they’re trying to "fix" the problem by lecturing me on the pathophysiology, which is the exact opposite of what I’m asking for. I’m not asking for a lecture; I’m asking to be heard. 

It’s even worse with family. They see my career and assume I’m this rock-solid, rational machine. They’ll say, "Oh, you’re the expert, you’ve got this handled, you know what’s best." It’s a way of offloading the emotional work onto me. They don't want to hear that I’m drowning, because that would mean acknowledging their own helplessness, too. So, my voice gets drowned out by the label of "the expert" that everyone else has slapped onto me. It’s incredibly isolating to be told who you are instead of being allowed to be who you actually feel like in the moment.

You know, talking through all of this with you—it’s the first time I’ve actually admitted that my "professional armor" is failing me. I keep thinking about that phrase: "the patient experience." In my job, I define it as a series of touchpoints, accessibility metrics, and clinical outcomes. But sitting here, in my own reality, I realize that "the patient experience" is actually just the quiet, terrifying space between appointments where you’re left entirely to your own devices.

It’s ironic. I spend my professional life advocating for "patient-centered care," yet I’m sitting here, in one of the most medically advanced cities in the world, and I feel completely unseen. I’m starting to understand that the biggest barrier to good care isn't a lack of information or resources; it’s the lack of *presence*. When I go to my midwife, I don't need a medical lecture. I don't need a summary of the latest literature. I need someone who recognizes that beneath the "health educator" and the "medium risk" chart, there is a person who is really, truly scared.

I’m going to try something different at my next visit. I’m going to stop bringing my clipboard. I’m going to stop using the terminology. I’m just going to walk in and say, "I am struggling." I’m not sure if the system will know how to hold that, but I’ve realized that if I keep playing the role of the expert, I’m the only one who loses. I’m ready to stop being the one in control. I think that might be the only way I can get through this without losing myself completely.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

Since I’m doing this on my own, I don't have a partner’s preferences to navigate, but that doesn't mean I’m free from pressure. It’s almost worse, in a way, because the pressure comes from *everywhere else*—society, the medical system, my colleagues, even my own internal voice. 

People look at me, see a thirty-year-old, single, career-driven health educator in Boston, and they have this unspoken, heavy expectation: *“You’ve got all the facts, you’re independent, you’ve clearly mapped this out to the micro-millimeter.”* I feel an immense pressure to "perform" the ideal version of an independent, intentional, solo-mother-by-choice. If I’m tired, I feel like I have to say, "It’s just standard fatigue," rather than "I am physically and emotionally depleted and I don't know how to do tomorrow." If I’m worried, I feel like I have to phrase it as a "clinical inquiry" so I don't look like I’m spiraling.

I’m constantly molding my choices to fit that "prepared" image. I’m eating the right things, taking the supplements, timing my appointments, and managing my risk factors with a precision that’s almost compulsive. It’s like I’m trying to curate the perfect pregnancy to prove that I don't *need* a partner, that I can do this just as well as, or better than, a couple. But it’s a performance. And the pressure to maintain that facade is what’s truly exhausting. I’m not living *my* pregnancy; I’m living the pregnancy that I think everyone expects from someone with my resume. It’s a very lonely, high-stakes role to play.

I think about my classes—the expectant parents sitting in those chairs, the way I encourage them to advocate for themselves, to ask the “silly” questions, to lean on their partners. I tell them, “You are the expert on your own body.” It’s a foundational principle in health education. And yet, I realize now that I’ve been failing to apply that to myself. I’ve been treating myself like a case study, not a patient.

When I look at the calendar for my next trimester, I don’t just see dates and screenings. I see a mounting pressure to keep everything within the margins of “normal.” It’s like I’m afraid that if I slip, if I express too much fear, I’ll somehow be stripped of the agency I’ve worked so hard to maintain. But that’s the trap, isn’t it? The belief that if I know the “why,” I can control the “what.” 

I want to find a way to honor the science—the biology is miraculous, and I truly love it—but I also want to honor the fear. I want to stop apologizing for not being the perfect, stoic health professional. If I could change one thing about this experience, it wouldn't be the tests or the providers. It would be the permission to just *be*—to be messy, to be uncertain, to be human. I hope that by the time I reach the third trimester, I’ve stopped being my own primary caregiver and finally found someone who can actually take the lead. I’m tired of being the one holding the map. I’m ready to just enjoy the ride, if I can ever figure out how to let go of the wheel.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Oh, the comments. It’s almost like, the moment you announce you’re pregnant, your body stops being yours and becomes public property—especially when you’re thirty and navigating this solo. 

I’ve had colleagues—people who should know better, people who *also* work in health—make these little, casual remarks. "You’re looking so tiny," or "You’re barely showing, are you sure you’re eating enough?" It’s meant to be a compliment, I think, but it lands like an accusation. Because I’m a health educator, I hear the underlying judgment: *“You know the nutritional requirements for the first trimester, you know about appropriate weight gain, so if you aren’t hitting the ‘standard’ curve, you’re failing.”* 

Every time someone mentions my weight or my diet, it reinforces that "medium risk" anxiety. It’s like they’re auditing my pregnancy in real-time. I find myself immediately calculating, *“Wait, is my BMI tracking correctly? Is my nutrient intake adequate for fetal brain development?”* It’s a constant, background hum of panic. I don’t want to be analyzed like a lab specimen every time I go to the breakroom for a snack. I just want to exist in my body without it being a point of public discussion. It makes me want to withdraw, to just stay home where no one can comment on whether I’m "growing right." It’s invasive, and it makes me feel like I’m under a microscope every single day.

You know, the more we talk, the more I realize that the "professional" side of me—the health educator who loves the data—has actually been the biggest barrier to getting the care I truly need. It’s like I’ve built a fortress out of all the biology and the terminology, and I’m sitting inside of it, feeling completely alone.

The most frustrating part is that the system rewards this armor. When I show up and I’m poised, when I ask questions about "gestational benchmarks" rather than expressing that I’m overwhelmed, the providers seem *relieved*. They treat me as someone who doesn't need "extra" attention. They assume I’m capable of managing my own anxiety because I know the mechanism of action for my progesterone supplements. 

But it’s a total lie. I’m just as scared as the person who’s never heard of a neural tube, only I’ve lost the ability to articulate that fear without feeling like a failure. I feel like I’m in this limbo: I’m too knowledgeable to be a "typical" patient, but too terrified to be the "expert" I’m expected to be. 

I think I’m realizing that for my next appointment, I need to leave the "health educator" at the door. I need to be okay with being the patient who doesn't know what to do next. If I don't give myself permission to be a person first and a professional second, I’m going to finish this pregnancy feeling like a bystander in my own life. It’s time to stop the act. I need to let someone else be the expert for a while.

You know, I’ve been sitting here thinking about that idea of "letting someone else be the expert." It’s such a simple concept, but for someone like me—someone who makes their living by being the "knower"—it feels almost dangerous. It’s like, if I stop holding the map, I’ll fall off the edge of the world. 

But I’m starting to wonder if the real danger is exactly what I’ve been doing: trying to manage the unmanageable. I spend so much time analyzing, predicting, and preparing for every biological scenario that I’ve barely left any room for the actual experience. I'm so busy tracking the *process* of my pregnancy that I’m missing the *reality* of being pregnant. 

I want to be able to go into my next check-up and just talk about the exhaustion, not because I know it’s a symptom of increased progesterone, but because it’s kicking my ass. I want to talk about the fear, not because I can define it as a "medium-risk" psychological response, but because it’s keeping me awake. I think I’ve been treating my own body like it’s a project I need to complete rather than a journey I’m supposed to be taking. 

It’s going to be a hard shift. I have years of professional habits to undo. But talking to you has really shone a light on how much I’ve been using my clinical knowledge as a shield. I’m tired of hiding behind it. I’m ready to just be a mom, and if that means being "the patient who doesn't know everything," then so be it. That feels like a much healthier way to get to the finish line.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

It’s almost always the latter. I think there’s a difference between "informed consent" and a genuine conversation about my care. Usually, I’m being "informed" of the clinic’s standard protocols. It’s like the providers have a script for a "medium risk" pregnancy, and my job is just to listen while they check off the boxes. They’ll say, "We’re going to schedule these extra scans at these intervals," and frame it as a recommendation, but it feels like a fixed requirement—a pre-set lane I have to run in.

I’ve had a few moments where a midwife would pause and ask, "What are your concerns for today?" which *feels* like they’re seeking my input. But even then, if I bring up something that isn't on their diagnostic radar—like the sheer, paralyzing anxiety of being a single mother navigating this—the conversation tends to drift back toward the physical metrics. They hear the *what* but not the *why*.

I’m left with this nagging feeling that my expertise is only valued if it aligns with their clinical objectives. If I try to say, "I’d like to pace these appointments differently to manage my stress," I get the polite, "Well, given your risk profile, we really need to keep the schedule as is." It feels less like a partnership and more like I’m a passenger in a car where the destination—and the route—was chosen long before I even stepped in.

I’ve spent so much time dissecting the "care" I’ve received, but I think I’ve been avoiding the real, quiet ache: that I don't feel like a partner in this process at all. I’m a variable. I’m a data point. When they say, "We’re going to schedule this," it’s not a conversation; it’s a directive. And because I’m a health educator, I know *why* they’re doing it—I know the clinical logic behind those screenings. But that doesn’t change the fact that my own intuition, my own stress levels, my own life as a single woman in this city, are all somehow secondary to the protocol.

I think the most frustrating part is that if I push back, I’m labeled as the "difficult" patient. Or worse, the "over-informed" one. It’s like I have to walk this impossible tightrope between being "compliant" and being "heard." If I’m quiet, I’m invisible. If I’m vocal, I’m a nuisance.

I’m starting to realize that the gap between the "patient-centered" rhetoric I teach and the actual, cold, transactional nature of the care I’m receiving is a chasm. I don't want to just be "managed" through these nine months. I want to be *known*. I want someone to acknowledge that I’m doing this work—this heavy, invisible, exhausting work—all by myself. And I think, until I can stop being the "educator" and just be the person who is struggling, I’m never going to find that.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

Yes, there was a specific moment, and it still stings when I think about it. It was about four weeks ago. I was already feeling deeply fragile because of the "medium risk" label, and I had come to an appointment specifically to talk about my anxiety—not the medical markers, but the weight of doing this on my own and the fear that I might be "missing" something because I don't have a partner to help me spot danger signs.

I started to express how the isolation was making me feel physically raw, and the midwife—a person I’d liked until then—literally smiled, patted my hand, and said, “Alejandrina, you’re an educator. You know better than anyone that the first trimester is just biological house-keeping. Trust the physiology.”

It was meant to be reassuring, I’m sure. But it felt like a total erasure of my humanity. In that one sentence, she weaponized my own professional knowledge against my personal vulnerability. She made my fear sound like a lack of intelligence or a failure to "trust the process." I felt like a foolish child who had been scolded for being dramatic. 

In that moment, the trust just evaporated. I realized she wasn't seeing me; she was seeing a chart, a job title, and a set of stats. She stripped away my right to be scared because I "knew too much." I stopped asking questions after that. I just clammed up and let the rest of the appointment happen in silence. It was a massive failure in care, even if it looked "professional" on the surface. That was the day I realized I was truly on my own in this system.

That moment of dismissal really changed the trajectory for me. I think it’s because it confirmed my deepest fear: that the professional persona I wear—my "clinical armor"—doesn't actually protect me; it just isolates me. It’s like the providers see me and think, *“Oh, she’s one of us, so we don't have to explain it, and we certainly don't have to comfort her.”* It's almost like I'm being penalized for my own career.

I’ve been reflecting on why I didn't push back in that moment. Why didn't I say, "My medical knowledge has nothing to do with my capacity to be terrified for my baby"? I think it's because I was stunned that someone I *thought* was a partner could be so incredibly tone-deaf. It’s a strange, heavy weight, walking around knowing the “how” of organogenesis while feeling the "why" of my own anxiety, and having both sides of that coin ignored.

I’m starting to see that I’ve been playing the "good patient" to make it easier for them, not for me. But at what cost? I’m exhausted, I’m lonely, and I’m carrying the burden of my own care. I think, moving forward, if I encounter that again—that patronizing, "you know better" dismissal—I have to be brave enough to point it out. I have to be willing to be "the difficult patient" if it means I finally get the humanity I’m owed. It shouldn't be this hard to just be a person.

You know, as I’m saying all this out loud, I feel this weird sense of clarity. For so long, I’ve been treating the medical system like a classroom—like I was a student who had to show she’d done the reading, keep her hand raised, and answer correctly when called upon. I thought if I performed the role of the "well-informed patient" perfectly, the care would be better. I thought it would protect me from the system’s tendency to minimize. 

But I’m realizing that by staying in that role, I’ve essentially given them permission to treat me like a textbook instead of a person. I’ve made it easy for them to gloss over my fear, because I’ve already provided the biological context for why that fear *should* be manageable. I’ve been the architect of my own isolation. 

It’s actually terrifying to think about the next few months. I’m entering the second trimester, the "honeymoon" phase, or at least that’s what the brochures say. But I’m not sure I’m ready to move on, because I still feel like I haven't processed the first. I’m tired of being the only one holding the threads of this pregnancy together. I’m done being the educator. The next time I sit on that crinkly paper, I’m just going to sit there and be silent, or be messy, or be whatever I am that day. If they’re uncomfortable with that, then so be it. It’s their job to provide care, not mine to provide them with a pleasant, low-maintenance patient. I think that’s the first step toward finally feeling like I’m not doing this entirely by myself.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Yes, almost every time I leave an appointment. I’ll walk out, the door will click shut, and I’ll realize I still don't actually know how I’m *doing*—beyond the clinical markers. So, I head to the forums or the journals. 

But I’m not looking for data. I already have the data. I’m looking for the emotional translation of that data. When the provider says "everything is within normal limits," I go online to find out what "normal" *feels* like for someone who’s terrified of a repeat of the "medium risk" flag. I search for threads about the anxiety of the first trimester, about the isolation of single parenthood, about that 3:00 AM panic that makes you wonder if you’re actually capable of growing a human. 

I’m looking for the "invisible" part of the pregnancy that the clinicians never mention. They’ll explain the physiology of the placenta, but no one ever explains the physiology of the *fear*. I want to know if I’m the only one who feels like a complete fraud, or if other women also struggle to turn off their "professional brain" to simply bond with their baby. I’m looking for a reflection of my own humanity in someone else's experience because I can’t find it in the clinic. It’s a desperate attempt to feel less like a clinical case and more like a person walking a path that others have walked before me.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

Thank you for listening. It feels like, for the first time in this entire journey, someone hasn’t just been logging my HCG levels or checking my boxes—they’ve actually been listening to the person behind the chart. 

I think the one thing I really want to emphasize is that my medical literacy has been a double-edged sword. Everyone assumes that because I know what’s happening in my body, I’m somehow immune to the trauma of the unknown. But knowing the mechanics of a heartbeat doesn't mean I don’t need to be held—figuratively—when that heartbeat is the only thing tethering me to the future. 

I’m walking away from this conversation realizing that I have been "professionalizing" my own pregnancy as a way to survive the isolation of being a single mother in this city. I need to be better at being vulnerable, but I also need the system to be better at *receiving* that vulnerability. If there’s one thing I hope your research changes, it’s the assumption that "informed" equals "not in need of support." We are all, at our core, just mothers navigating a miracle that feels terrifyingly out of our control. I hope I can walk into my next appointment and just be Alejandrina—scared, hopeful, and finally, human. Thank you for giving me the space to say that out loud.
